name: Nerja, Frigiliana, and Torrox Loop
name_original: Acantilados de Maro-Cerro Gordo — ida y vuelta por Frigiliana
description: This difficult 55.4-mile road cycling route offers dramatic coastal views, challenging climbs, and charming white villages like Frigiliana.
region_intro: Road cycling around Almuñécar offers diverse terrain, transitioning from extensive coastal promenades to challenging mountainous hinterlands. The region is characterized by a unique microclimate, providing smooth road surfaces and varied elevations. Cyclists can experience rides from sea level along the Mediterranean coast to alpine-like elevations in the foothills of the Sierra Nevada and Sierra de Tejeda, Almijara y Alhama mountain ranges. This varied landscape provides options for different fitness levels, including significant climbs and scenic coastal routes. Best road cycling routes around Almuñécar The most popular road cycling route is Cliffs of Maro-Cerro Gordo — round trip via Frigiliana, a 55.4 miles (89.2 km) trail that takes 4 hours 54 minutes to complete. This difficult route features dramatic coastal cliffs and views towards mountain villages. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Carretera de la Cabra – Recta de la Cabra loop from Almuñécar, a difficult 76.9 miles (123.8 km) path. This iconic climb offers spectacular views over numerous switchbacks as it ascends to higher elevations. Local road cyclists also love the El Peñoncillo Viewpoint – N-340 quiet & fast coast road loop from La Herradura, a 38.0 miles (61.2 km) trail leading through coastal roads and offering sea viewpoints, often completed in about 2 hours 54 minutes. Road cycling around Almuñécar is defined by coastal promenades, mountainous hinterland, and the foothills of the Sierra Nevada. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate coastal rides to advanced climbs with significant elevation difficulty. The routes in Almuñécar are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.1 stars from over 100 reviews. More than 5,000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Almuñécar's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Almuñécar?
A: There are over 35 road cycling routes around Almuñécar, catering to various skill levels. You'll find 5 easy routes, 19 moderate routes, and 11 challenging routes, ensuring options for every cyclist.

Q: What kind of terrain and difficulty can I expect on road cycling routes in Almuñécar?
A: Almuñécar offers a diverse road cycling experience, from extensive coastal promenades to challenging mountainous hinterlands. Most rides from the town involve climbs, leading to exhilarating descents. You can expect smooth road surfaces and varied elevations, with options ranging from moderate coastal rides to advanced climbs with significant elevation difficulty, such as the iconic Puerto de la Cabra.

Q: Which road cycling routes offer the best scenic views or viewpoints?
A: Many routes provide stunning vistas. For dramatic coastal cliffs and views towards mountain villages, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e981410528/cliffs-of-maro-cerro-gordo-round-trip-via-frigiliana" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cliffs of Maro-Cerro Gordo — round trip via Frigiliana&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/3954308" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Carretera de la Cabra – Recta de la Cabra loop from Almuñécar&lt;/a&gt; offers spectacular views over numerous switchbacks as it ascends. You can also find excellent viewpoints like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/711229" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Sierra de Almijara&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6384695" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Sierra de Tejeda Mountains&lt;/a&gt; along mountain pass routes.

Q: Are there any particularly challenging road cycling climbs in the Almuñécar area?
A: Yes, Almuñécar is known for its challenging climbs. The "Puerto de la Cabra" is a renowned ascent, offering approximately 35 km of climbing with an average slope of 7%. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/3954308" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Carretera de la Cabra – Recta de la Cabra loop from Almuñécar&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 123.8 km path that includes this iconic climb. Other challenging options include routes through the Sierra de la Almijara and the climb to Peña Escrita at 1,200 meters.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Almuñécar?
A: Almuñécar benefits from a unique 'Costa Tropical' microclimate, making it suitable for year-round outdoor activities. This means you can enjoy road cycling comfortably for most of the year, avoiding the extreme heat of mid-summer in some other Spanish regions and benefiting from mild winters.

Q: Can I find road cycling routes that stick to the coast?
A: Absolutely. While Almuñécar's immediate vicinity involves some climbing, you can find scenic coastal rides. Routes along Playa Velilla, towards La Herradura, or extended rides towards Nerja or Motril, often passing through the natural park of Maro and Cerro Gordo, offer beautiful sea views. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39561551" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Peñoncillo Viewpoint – N-340 quiet & fast coast road loop from La Herradura&lt;/a&gt; is a great example of a coastal route.

Q: Are there any family-friendly road cycling options around Almuñécar?
A: While many road cycling routes in Almuñécar involve significant climbs, some flatter sections along the coast, particularly further from the town center, might be more suitable for families or less experienced cyclists. Always check the route's elevation profile and difficulty before setting out with family members.

Q: What historical or natural landmarks can I see while road cycling near Almuñécar?
A: The region is rich in attractions. You might cycle past the Roman Aqueduct, the historic Castillo de San Miguel, or the Old Town area. Natural features include the dramatic cliffs of the Maro-Cerro Gordo Natural Park. For specific natural highlights, consider routes that pass by &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/381716" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Higuerón River Gorge and Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/87627" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Verde Waterfalls&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there many circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes around Almuñécar are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/3954308" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Carretera de la Cabra – Recta de la Cabra loop from Almuñécar&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39561551" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Peñoncillo Viewpoint – N-340 quiet & fast coast road loop from La Herradura&lt;/a&gt;. These loops offer varied scenery without needing to retrace your path.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Almuñécar?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.1 stars from over 100 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse terrain, from coastal roads to challenging mountain climbs, the unique microclimate, and the stunning views over the Mediterranean and the Sierra Nevada foothills. The smooth road surfaces and often minimal traffic are also frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there opportunities for coffee stops or refreshments along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes in the Almuñécar region pass through charming whitewashed villages. These villages often provide excellent opportunities for a coffee stop, a quick snack, or a meal, allowing you to refuel and experience local culture during your ride.

Q: What kind of elevation gain can I expect on road cycling routes in Almuñécar?
A: Road cycling in Almuñécar often involves significant elevation gain due to its location between the sea and mountains. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e981410528/cliffs-of-maro-cerro-gordo-round-trip-via-frigiliana" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cliffs of Maro-Cerro Gordo — round trip via Frigiliana&lt;/a&gt; route has over 1,800 meters of ascent, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/3954308" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Carretera de la Cabra – Recta de la Cabra loop from Almuñécar&lt;/a&gt; features over 2,200 meters of climbing. Even moderate routes can include several hundred meters of elevation.
tips: Cape that appears on the coast of Los Acantilados de Maro-Cerro Gordo and where the cape offers cliffs next to the Mediterranean coast. Beautiful road full of views.

The aqueduct Águila (translated as the eagle) in Nerja is a very large aqueduct and immediately catches the eye. 37 arches made of brick span four floors. In total, the aqueduct is around forty meters high. The water supply comes from an open canal on the top floor.

The entire building is reminiscent of the Mudejar style typical of Andalusia, especially on its northern side. The Mudéjares were assimilated Muslims who were allowed to remain in Spain after Arab rule was pushed back from the 11th to 15th centuries.
Very nice mirador that offers a beautiful view of the cliffs and the small beaches. Wonderful views, a great experience! Just take a short stop and capture the perspectives.
Ascent that goes from the town of Torrox to the highest part of the Lomas de las Coscoja, towards Frigiliana. About four kilometers at about six percent percentage.
Frigiliana - one of the most beautiful white villages in Andalusia. The Múdejar neighborhood is classified as an Asset of Cultural Interest.
Nice and wide coastal road with beautiful views.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best times to cycle this route are typically spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, the weather is generally mild and pleasant for cycling, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the cooler, potentially wetter days of winter. This allows for comfortable enjoyment of both the coastal and inland sections.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Cliffs of Maro-Cerro Gordo — round trip via Frigiliana?
faqs.answer: The terrain is characterized by significant ascents and descents, typical of Andalusia's mountainous region. You should expect substantial climbing, with some sections being steep and 'front-loaded'. Road surfaces are generally well-paved, especially on main routes, though care is needed on steep descents and tight turns. The route is designed for racebikes, indicating a focus on paved roads.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is primarily a road cycling tour on public roads. While there are no specific restrictions against dogs on public roads, it is generally not recommended to bring dogs on a challenging 89 km racebike route with significant elevation changes due to safety concerns for both the dog and the cyclist. If you do bring a dog, ensure it is safely secured and able to handle the distance and conditions.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or is there an entrance fee for the Paraje Natural Acantilados de Maro-Cerro Gordo?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need a permit or pay an entrance fee to cycle through the Paraje Natural Acantilados de Maro-Cerro Gordo on this route. The cycling path follows public roads that traverse the natural park, allowing free access to enjoy the stunning coastal scenery.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling tour?
faqs.answer: While the exact starting point for parking isn't specified, common access points for this region include towns like Nerja or Frigiliana. You would typically look for public parking areas within these towns. For example, parking in Frigiliana would allow you to start directly from the charming white village.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: This route offers a variety of scenic and cultural highlights. You'll experience breathtaking coastal views from the View from Cerro Gordo and El Peñoncillo Viewpoint. Inland, you'll pass through the picturesque white village of Frigiliana and can admire the historic Eagle Aqueduct (Acueducto del Águila). The route also includes a notable Climb from Torrox to the Lomas de las Coscoja and sections of the N-340 quiet & fast coast road.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginner road cyclists?
faqs.answer: No, this route is graded as 'difficult' and involves significant elevation gain (over 1800m) across nearly 90 km. It is best suited for experienced road cyclists with good fitness levels who are comfortable with challenging climbs and descents. Beginners might find the sustained effort and steep sections too demanding.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route incorporates sections of several other paths. You will find yourself on parts of the Valle de Frigiliana, La Senda Litoral, Torrox - Nerja, Ruta Acebuchal, Ruta Venta Cebolleros, Ruta La Lastra, Cómpeta - Frigiliana, and Frigiliana - Cómpeta, among others, as you navigate the area.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect on this tour?
faqs.answer: The scenery is incredibly diverse and dramatic. You'll experience stunning coastal views with steep cliffs plunging into the Mediterranean Sea in the Maro-Cerro Gordo area. As you head inland towards Frigiliana and the Sierra de Almijara mountains, the landscape transitions to rugged hills, charming white villages, and expansive views that, on clear days, can even include the Atlas Mountains of Morocco. Many inland roads are peaceful with minimal traffic.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or get refreshments along the way, especially in Frigiliana?
faqs.answer: Yes, Frigiliana is a well-known white village with a good selection of cafes and restaurants where you can stop for refreshments, a meal, or to refill water bottles. Given its popularity, it's an ideal spot for a break during your ride. Other towns along the coast, like Nerja or Torrox, would also offer options.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this road cycling tour?
faqs.answer: This challenging 89 km route, with over 1800 meters of elevation gain, typically takes around 5 hours of active cycling time. However, including stops for breaks, photos, and exploring villages like Frigiliana, you should plan for a full day's outing.

waypoints.name: Climb from Torrox to the Lomas de las Coscoja
